summary: Joel Spolsky and Jeff Atwood discuss the diminishing returns of strict unit testing and overly rigid object-oriented design principles like SOLID. Joel argues that 100% unit test coverage creates a maintenance burden that grows disproportionately as codebases evolve, since design changes routinely break a constant percentage of tests. He also criticizes the SOLID principles, particularly Interface Segregation and Single Responsibility, as bureaucratic over-engineering that produces millions of tiny classes nobody needs. Both conclude that what ultimately matters is delivering value to customers quickly, and that dogmatic adherence to any methodology can become counterproductive friction.
key_points: Unit test maintenance costs grow disproportionately as projects get bigger, because design changes routinely break a constant percentage of tests that must then be rewritten.
Unit tests are most valuable for stable, low-churn codebases like compilers or public APIs, where the design is fixed and breakage is catastrophic.
The SOLID principles, taken literally, lead to an explosion of tiny classes and interfaces that cost enormous time to build but provide little practical benefit for internal code.
Pragmatic software development means building imperfect things that work well, then fixing problems as they arise rather than engineering defensively against hypothetical future changes.
The ultimate measure of software quality is how quickly you can respond to real business needs and deliver value to customers, not adherence to design principles.
money_quotes: The whole idea of agile programming is not to do things before you need them, but to page-fault them in as needed.

The amount of investment you'll have to make in maintaining those unit tests, keeping them up-to-date and keeping them passing, starts to become disproportional to the amount of benefit that you get out of them.
People that say things like this have just never written a heck of a lot of code.

You're spending a lot of time in advance writing code that is just not going to be relevant, it's not going to be important. It could, theoretically, protect you against things, but, how about waiting until those things happen before you protect yourself against them?
The way real software works is that you create these very imperfect things, and they work great.
The unit of measure that really matters to me is, how quickly you can respond to real business needs with your code.
The worst thing that happens is that you get people that just stop thinking about what they're doing.
Quality really doesn't matter that much, in the big scheme of things.
key_insight: Dogmatic adherence to unit testing and OO design principles like SOLID creates an ever-growing maintenance burden that, for most internal codebases, costs more than it saves — pragmatic developers should invest in engineering rigor proportionally to actual risk rather than following rules for their own sake.
